findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) + ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset + ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 + ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've nailed the market opportunity, but investors will want to know this problem actually keeps builders and homeowners up at night—add a slide showing 3-4 customer discovery moments like "interviewed 12 villa developers in Dubai who cited reliability as their #1 blocker to solar adoption" or "collected letters of intent from 3 major developers representing 200+ units" or even "ran a survey of 50 GCC property owners showing 78% would switch to solar if systems didn't require constant maintenance." This transforms your mandate-and-failure story from logical to lived.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they're sitting at the bottom like supporting facts rather than the reason this moment is critical—add a timeline that shows the urgency. For example, add a line like "Estidama Phase 3 (2026) mandates solar hot water on all new villas—creating a 24-month window before enforcement tightens" or "UAE Climate Law targets 30% renewable energy by 2030, making 2024-2025 the peak procurement window for developers planning 2026-2028 builds." This transforms "regulations exist" into "the market is about to shift and we're positioned to capture it now."

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature roadmap, not a defensible technology. Add one concrete example showing how your AI actually works — for instance, walk through a real scenario: "A partner in Cairo inputs a villa's roof dimensions and hot water demand; HelioHelp outputs the exact collector configuration, installation sequence, and 25-year carbon savings projection in under 2 minutes, versus our team taking 3 days." This shows investors the actual intelligence layer, not just the output categories.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: You've built HelioHelp as a core differentiator, but investors will want to know what's under the hood — specifically, which cloud infrastructure (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure?), AI/ML frameworks (OpenAI APIs, custom models?), or third-party services power it, and what happens if any of them become unavailable or change pricing. Add a brief technical dependencies section to Slide 19 that lists your core external dependencies (e.g., "Cloud: AWS for model inference and data storage; AI: Custom PyTorch models + OpenAI API for knowledge engine") and one sentence on your mitigation strategy (e.g., "Multi-region deployment and in-house model

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi, GCC) with mandated solar installations and utility partnerships, but investors won't see how you're handling data security, system certifications, or compliance requirements—especially critical since HelioHelp collects building data and performance metrics globally. Add one bullet to Slide 5 (Technology) under HelioPro stating something like "ISO 27001 certified data architecture + GDPR/local data residency compliance for global deployments" so investors know you've built compliance into your platform from day one, not bolted it on later.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue, gross profit, and EBITDA figures, and mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and 'GTM via developers/ESCOs ensures scalable revenue from 2026,' but do not explicitly state growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-line assumptions box to your financial table that spells out exactly how you're getting from $213K contracted to $1.05M in 2027 revenue—for example: "2027 assumes 50 installations at $21K avg hardware ASP + 15% attach rate on licensing, with 3-4 month sales cycle via developer partnerships." Right now investors can't reverse-engineer your numbers, which makes them skeptical even though your pipeline is real.

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see CAC and LTV to understand how efficiently you're acquiring customers and whether the unit economics actually work at scale. Add a simple table showing: for hotels, what's your average deal size ($207K for Rixos?), how much you spend to land that customer, and what the lifetime value is based on the O&M contracts you mentioned—this will prove the 38–46% margin translates into a repeatable, profitable playbook.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy of $87,000 between the contracted amount and projected hardware revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted on Slide 12 is your actual near-term revenue locked in, while the $300K on Slide 14 is your 2026 projection—clarify this by adding a note on Slide 14 saying "2026 Hardware Revenue includes $213K contracted (Rixos + EtihadWE) plus $87K from pipeline discussions (Abu Dhabi Ports, ALDAR, Masdar City expected closures)" so investors see how you bridge from certain to projected revenue, not a math error.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: You've shown strong pipeline and unit economics, but investors need to see exactly how you'll deploy this new capital to hit those 2026-2027 targets. Add a dedicated slide breaking down: how much goes to manufacturing scale-up (tooling, inventory), sales/BD team hiring, installation capacity, and working capital—then tie each line item directly to a specific milestone (e.g., "$80K for injection molding equipment to achieve 55% margins by Q3 2026" or "$60K for two field installers to handle the Rixos + EtihadWE deployments"). This transforms your financials from a projection into a credible execution plan.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line under Rixos and EtihadWE stating the payment status explicitly — something like "Rixos: $180K received Q3 2024" or "EtihadWE: Monthly recurring revenue $12K since launch" — so investors see actual cash in, not just project names. Right now it reads like a client list, not proof of revenue.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: Right now your traction slide shows capacity and partnerships, but investors need to see actual revenue movement—add a simple chart showing your monthly revenue for the last 6-8 months (even if it's small) with the month-over-month percentage growth called out, like "Jan: $12K → Feb: $18K (+50%) → Mar: $24K (+33%)". This proves the business is accelerating, not just that you have potential.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer (Rixos) that survived a severe storm while competitors' systems failed—that's your retention story, so lean into it hard. Add a metric like "100% system uptime post-deployment" or "zero churn across installed base" to your traction slide, and explicitly call out in your pitch that this durability advantage directly translates to customer stickiness and repeat business (the PO for 570 collectors proves the customer came back).

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Replace "Legacy Solar," "Heat Pumps," and "Electric Storage" with actual competitor names—something like "Sunrun Solar," "Carrier Heat Pumps," and "Tesla Powerwall"—so investors immediately understand who you're beating and can validate your claims against real products they know. Right now the table reads as theoretical; naming real competitors makes it credible and memorable.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why SolarisKit wins against existing solutions, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Carrier, or a well-funded solar company decides to build exactly what you're building. Add a section called "Why Incumbents Can't Compete" that explains one structural reason they won't move fast (e.g., "Heat pump makers are locked into outdoor-unit architectures by 50 years of supply chain investment" or "Solar companies' flat-roof expertise is in panels, not thermal systems—different physics, different teams"). This shows you've thought beyond the current competitive set.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing a chance to leverage outside validation—add a small "Advisors" section naming 1-2 people with relevant expertise (ideally someone from solar/cleantech, someone from manufacturing or scale-up) with their title and one-line credential, like "John Smith, Former VP Operations at [major solar company]" or "Sarah Chen, Board Member at [relevant cleantech fund]." This signals you've thought strategically about gaps and have trusted experts in your corner, which investors find reassuring.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your Team slide stating the vesting schedule for you and your co-founders—something like "Founder equity: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" or whatever your actual terms are. Investors always ask this in the room, and showing you've thought it through upfront (and that you're locked in long-term) builds confidence that you're serious and aligned with them.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You're mentioning Solar Keymark certification on Slide 3, but investors in regulated markets need to see the full compliance picture upfront—add a dedicated compliance slide before the financials that maps which specific UAE building codes (Estidama, Dubai Municipality, Barjeel) your system meets, which certifications you already hold (Solar Keymark, ISO/EN), and which you're pursuing by when (e.g., "Dubai Municipality approval: Q2 2026"). This turns the regulatory tailwind from a market driver into proof that you've eliminated adoption friction for your actual customers.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked down and a strong risk mitigation slide, but investors will want explicit clarity on whether there are any pending disputes, claims, or encumbrances tied to your IP or operations—especially given you're manufacturing in the UAE and have global patent filings. Add a single bullet to your IP section on Slide 8 or create a brief legal status note stating something like "No pending litigation, IP disputes, or material encumbrances; all patents granted and in good standing across US, EU, and UK jurisdictions" so there's zero ambiguity when due diligence begins.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these align. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing how the 5% UAE market share assumption ($20M) connects to your $400M SAM figure—for example: "5% of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity" or "Targeting 5% of addressable villa/developer segment by 2030." Right now investors can't verify the math, which makes the whole market sizing feel disconnected even though your numbers are solid.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, and Slide 3 labeled '[UNKNOWN]' suggests incomplete slide deck
findings.suggestion: Go through the entire deck systematically and replace every [URL_REDACTED] placeholder with the actual URL or remove the reference entirely—investors will notice these gaps immediately and assume the deck isn't final. For Slide 6, the cut-off text "Strong push for dec" should read "Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law)" to match the footnote below it. Rename Slide 3 from "[UNKNOWN]" to "The Solution" or "Product" so it reads as a finished, professional deck rather than a work-in-progress.
